specification.topic-statement: The Anthropic error handler maps HTTP status codes to standardized error categories for non-streaming Anthropic error responses, using the response body only for structural validation.

scope.in-scope: HTTP status code to error category mapping for non-streaming responses, JSON body parsing for structural validation, and the contract that category is determined solely by status code regardless of body content.
scope.boundary: The full set of error categories and their definitions, along with error categorization for Google and OpenAI providers, is covered by the provider error categorization specification.

Error categorization for errors delivered as in-stream SSE events uses a different mechanism — mapping the error "type" string rather than the HTTP status code — and is covered by the Anthropic streaming event processor specification.
How error categories are used downstream to decide whether to retry or abort a request is covered by the LLM response handling specification.

field.name: body
field.note: raw response body; must not be null

field.name: category
field.note: mapped error category written to out-parameter on success

field.name: result
field.note: OK on success; ERR(PARSE) if JSON body cannot be parsed

behavior.status-code-mapping:
The HTTP status code is mapped to an error category according to the following rules, applied in order:
- 401, 403 → AUTH - 429 → RATE_LIMIT - 400 → INVALID_ARG - 404 → NOT_FOUND - 500, 529 → SERVER (529 is the Anthropic-specific overloaded status) - All other codes → UNKNOWN
The category is written to the output parameter immediately after this mapping, before any body parsing occurs.
HTTP 529 is not a standard status code. Anthropic uses it to signal service overload, equivalent in meaning to a server-side error.
behavior.body-parsing:
After the category is determined, the response body is parsed as JSON. If parsing fails, a parse error is returned. On success, the root value is used to locate an "error" object. If the "error" object is present, its "type" and "message" fields are accessed but their values are discarded — they are used only to confirm the expected structure exists.
The error body JSON is parsed for structural validation only. The "type" and "message" string values within the error object are explicitly ignored for categorization purposes. The category assigned by the HTTP status code mapping is never modified by any field in the response body. This is the inverse of how Google and OpenAI error handling works, where body fields can refine or override the initial HTTP-based category.
If the JSON body cannot be parsed at all, the handler returns a parse error rather than falling back silently. The category output parameter will have been set by the time this failure occurs, but the caller receives an error result indicating the body was malformed.
behavior.success-return: When the body is valid JSON, the handler returns a success result. The category output parameter holds the value determined by the status code mapping and is ready for use by the caller regardless of whether the error object or its fields were present in the body.
